questions.question: State Ohm’s law and describe an experiment to verify it.
questions.answer: Ohm’s law states that the current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across its ends, provided temperature remains constant (V = IR). In the experiment, a circuit with a resistor, ammeter, voltmeter, rheostat, and battery is set up. By varying the potential difference and measuring current, a straight-line V–I graph is obtained, verifying Ohm’s law.

questions.question: Derive the expression for resistance of a conductor in terms of its resistivity, length, and cross-sectional area.
questions.answer: Resistance depends on length (L), cross-sectional area (A), and nature of material. It is directly proportional to length and inversely proportional to area. Thus R ∝ L/A. Introducing proportionality constant ρ (resistivity), R = ρL/A.

questions.question: Explain the equivalent resistance of resistors connected in series and derive the formula.
questions.answer: In a series circuit the same current flows through each resistor. If resistors R1, R2, and R3 are connected in series, the total potential difference is the sum of individual potential differences. Thus the equivalent resistance is R = R1 + R2 + R3.

questions.question: Explain the equivalent resistance of resistors connected in parallel and derive the formula.
questions.answer: In parallel combination, the potential difference across each resistor is the same but current divides. If R1, R2, and R3 are connected in parallel, the reciprocal of total resistance equals the sum of reciprocals of individual resistances: 1/R = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3.

questions.question: Compare series and parallel combinations of resistors with suitable examples.
questions.answer: In series combination the same current flows through all resistors and total resistance increases. In parallel combination the voltage is the same across each resistor and total resistance decreases. Household circuits use parallel combination so appliances work independently.

questions.question: Derive the relation between electric power, voltage, and current.
questions.answer: Electric power is the rate of doing electrical work. Work done to move charge Q through potential difference V is W = VQ. Since current I = Q/t, power P = W/t = VQ/t = VI.

questions.question: Derive two alternative expressions for electric power using Ohm’s law.
questions.answer: Power P = VI. Using Ohm’s law (V = IR), P = I(IR) = I²R. Also since I = V/R, P = V(V/R) = V²/R.

questions.question: State Joule’s law of heating and explain its applications.
questions.answer: Joule’s law states that heat produced in a conductor is proportional to the square of current, resistance, and time (H = I²Rt). Applications include electric heaters, electric irons, and electric kettles.

questions.question: Explain the heating effect of electric current with examples.
questions.answer: When electric current flows through a conductor, electrical energy converts into heat due to resistance. This is called the heating effect of current. It is used in devices such as electric heaters, irons, and toasters.

questions.question: Explain why tungsten is used as the filament in electric bulbs.
questions.answer: Tungsten has a very high melting point (about 3380°C) and high resistivity, enabling it to glow at high temperatures without melting.

questions.question: Explain why copper and aluminium are used for electrical wiring.
questions.answer: Copper and aluminium have low resistivity and high electrical conductivity, allowing current to flow easily with minimal heat loss.

questions.question: Describe the working and importance of a fuse in an electric circuit.
questions.answer: A fuse is a safety device made of a thin wire with low melting point. When excessive current flows, the fuse wire melts and breaks the circuit, protecting appliances from damage.

questions.question: Explain why household appliances are connected in parallel.
questions.answer: In parallel connection, each appliance receives the same voltage and can be switched on or off independently without affecting others.

questions.question: Explain how electric current flows through a metallic conductor.
questions.answer: Electric current flows through a metallic conductor due to the movement of free electrons when a potential difference is applied across the conductor.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between current, charge, and time.
questions.answer: Electric current is defined as the rate of flow of charge. The relation is I = Q/t, where Q is charge and t is time.

questions.question: Explain why resistivity is considered a characteristic property of materials.
questions.answer: Resistivity depends only on the nature of the material and temperature, not on the size or shape of the conductor, making it a characteristic property.

questions.question: Describe how the length and cross-sectional area of a conductor affect its resistance.
questions.answer: Resistance increases with increase in length and decreases with increase in cross-sectional area, according to R = ρL/A.

questions.question: Explain the concept of electrical energy consumption in households.
questions.answer: Electrical energy consumption refers to the total electrical energy used by appliances over time and is measured in kilowatt-hour (kWh).

questions.question: What is the commercial unit of electrical energy? Explain its significance.
questions.answer: The commercial unit of electrical energy is kilowatt-hour (kWh). It is used by electricity boards to measure and bill electrical energy consumption.

questions.question: Explain the role of an ammeter and a voltmeter in an electrical circuit.
questions.answer: An ammeter measures electric current and is connected in series, while a voltmeter measures potential difference and is connected in parallel across a component.

questions.question: Explain why an ammeter has very low resistance while a voltmeter has very high resistance.
questions.answer: An ammeter has very low resistance so it does not significantly affect the current in the circuit. A voltmeter has very high resistance so that it draws minimal current from the circuit.

questions.question: Explain how electrical energy is converted into other forms of energy in appliances.
questions.answer: Electrical energy converts into heat (heater, iron), light (bulb), mechanical energy (fan), and sound (speaker) depending on the appliance.

questions.question: Describe the importance of electricity in modern society.
questions.answer: Electricity is essential for lighting, heating, communication, transportation, industrial production, and operation of household appliances.

questions.question: Explain the significance of Ohm’s law in designing electrical circuits.
questions.answer: Ohm’s law helps determine the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance, allowing engineers to design safe and efficient electrical circuits.

questions.question: Explain how excessive current can damage electrical appliances and how safety devices prevent it.
questions.answer: Excessive current produces excessive heat due to resistance, which can damage wires and appliances. Safety devices such as fuses and circuit breakers break the circuit to prevent damage.
